transcript.transcript: Natvig up at Clagestone, he used to get about anything there at his place he wanted. What was his name? J.C. Natvig. Natvig. There, I guess, what is it, Mountain Home Music Retreat or something? Yeah. How did that name come about? That name hasn't always been on there, right? No. Some later person came along. I think Robbins put that on there. Isn't he the one that's got it now? Yeah, but that's been there. Well, he's the one that got that mountain music. Is that right? Yeah. I don't know if it even had a name before, other than just NatVig. How did the railroad figure in to this community? Well, I don't know just how it figured in. It was, came through here about 1907, I think. Did some of the places get supplies from the railroad? Oh yeah, they hauled in their supplies and it started hauling the mail. I don't know how they got the mail in this country before the railroad. At that time the railroad went through why they wasn't in existence up here. There was an old valley over here. It was the post office, and there were several stores there, a valley. And over the years, my mother and Three or two of her sisters carried the mail from where the train stopped at where they is now down here this Valley Coast office Valley that's right over there. Where they have that laundromat now? Yeah. There's the laundromat, just the other side of it. Yeah. One... There used to be an old structure of some sort on that corner, a tall... I think it was a water tower. There was a water tank there. Was that there when you came? Well, part of it was, it seems. Yeah, there was a big water tank there at one time. And that was what that tank was for, was to irrigate these garden spots. They thought they could raise a lot of truck gardening here. They had a big pump in there. Right over across the field here there was a pretty good sized sawmill. I don't know how big it was, but I can remember a sawdust pile there when I was a kid growing up. They said they had a pretty big lumberyard there and there was several stores and a post office, and quite a few people there. I don't know how many people lived around there. There was a building there. They said it was a hotel. It was not a hotel when you were growing up. It wasn't a hotel, no. People were living in it for a house when I was growing up. And right out here at the road, where our mailbox is, There was supposed to have been a saloon there, bar, whatever they want to call it. I remember seeing the well there when I was a kid growing up. The well was right there where the Reggie Road is now. And this county road, early days, I guess, come around out to the field out here. I can remember the telephone poles and the telephone line used to run through there when I was, well, after I was growing up, this is where they moved it out. Lots of changes. Yeah. In some ways. Probably, yeah. Lots of changes in some ways. Yeah, a couple, yeah. What about some of, are there lots of people here now that you've known all your life? Not really, no. There's probably four or five families in this whole area that I grew up with, or was here when I was growing up, there's Holquist and the Foxes, Whittems, Hazel's folks, they were here. The Bloods have been here quite a long time. And outside of that, I think that's about it. Where have the other people gone? Well, it was about here about the late 30s, everybody moved into town, it seemed like. A lot of these places around here, they moved into Spokane, different places. And then here, commenced about 25 years ago, and they started moving back. Do you think improved transportation had some bearing on that? It probably did. People who live here now, I guess we can work clear into Spokane, which isn't too far. 60 miles. Well, tell me something about your place in your family. You had some brothers and sisters, did you tell me? One brother, and he'd been dead for 16, 17 years. My dad died in 65, and my mother died in about 84, I think it was. I'm the last one. But I have quite a few cousins live around here. I still have two aunts living in Sand Point. But outside of that, not too much of my family left. You belong to the local Seventh-day Adventist church? Yes. Have you always been in that? I never have. I haven't always been in it, after Hazel and I were married. Was she always in the church? She was raised in that church. She was raised in it. She went to academy over there at Auburn, Washington. Q. What year did you tell me you were married again? A. 1947. Q. 1947. Maybe you didn't mention the year, but you said you were married in Spokane. You lived right here then. No, we lived in Montana. Oh, you lived in Montana? Yeah, I was in Montana. When you first got married. And what about your children? Well, we've got three daughters. Two of them are nurses. One lives in Lincoln, Nebraska, one in Arlington, Washington, and one here in Sandpoint. We've Got six grandchildren. That's a nice family. That's just none for her. They used these schoolhouses around here for everything, all kinds of meetings, church, grain to grange met in them for a long time, the Edgemere school for a long time, and mock socials and pie socials, whatever meeting they needed to have, why, they had them in the schoolhouse, dances. They had the funerals there. I don't know, just about everything took place, Took place in school houses. Did people who lived around the area get together for social things quite a bit, involving Quite a bit, yeah. A lot of people. I don't remember just any certain ones, but... We skipped around quite a bit, all different things. You mentioned that your people came here in 1893, or the original ones who owned this land. That was your great-great-uncle. My great-great-uncle had this place here. And then my great-grandparents, they came in 1993. They settled down here, about a mile and a half from here. They had a pretty big family, I think four boys and three daughters. Did all the sons and daughters then stay in this area for a while? No. They didn't? It was only one of Their daughters was here to begin with, and then I think one came later on and she left. But their sons all stayed here until they died, I think. They were all buried down here in the cemetery. All your family background, have they all been in this country for a long time? Well, since 1893 when my great-grandparents came here, and then my dad's came here in 1911. I meant, for example, did they come to Minnesota and into Canada from some other foreign country? They came from Germany, I believe. My mother's family came from Germany, and my dad's father came from Ireland. He left Ireland when he was 15 years old and joined the British merchant marine. He never went back to Ireland after that. William Kenny. And after he'd been in the merchant marine about 15 years, he came out to eastern Oregon and raised cattle and sheep there for probably 15 years, 16. And then they moved to Canada and was there about seven years, and then they moved back down here, bought this place here. And he lived here until he died. He died in 1928. Then my grandmother, in later years, she moved to Oregon. And that's where she died, in Oregon. Harry, I want to thank you so much for this interview. I really appreciate it. And this tape will be placed in the collection at the North Idaho College for researchers who are looking for information on those early days. Well, I'm not going to give you much help. Oh, I think it's great. Thank you. Yeah, I'm working tomorrow, what do you want? I'm working on a new movie. Really? Yeah. I'm working on a new movie. I'm working on a new movie. Oh, yeah. I'm really excited about that. I'm working on a new movie. All right. Yeah, I'll text you back. There you go. All right. OK. OK. All right. OK. OK. OK. OK. All right. All right. Okay. I'm going to pull it out. Okay. One. Two. Three. Four. Five. Six. Seven. Eight. Nine. Ten. Eleven. Twelve. Thirteen. Fourteen. Fifteen. Sixteen. Seventeen. Eighteen. Nineteen. Twenty-one. Twenty-two. Twenty-three. Twenty-four. Twenty-five. Twenty-six. Twenty-seven. So, I'm going to go ahead and get started. I'm going to go ahead and start with a demonstration of how to do a test. So, I'm going to start with a demonstration of how to do a Right. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. So, I'm going to go ahead and get started. So, I'm going to start with the first one. The first one is a little bit of a story. So, this is a story. This is a story about a guy who was in a car accident. He was driving a car. He was driving a car. He was driving a car. He was driving a car. He was driving a car. He was driving a car. Yeah. All right. We'll do that. OK. I think we're good. OK. That's good. OK. We're good. OK. OK. Yeah. OK. Yeah. OK. OK. Yeah. OK. OK. Yeah. OK. Yeah. OK. Yeah. Yeah. OK. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah.
